introSection:
Glenwood Caverns Adventure Park is a summer-themed adventure park located near Glenwood Springs, Colorado. It is located approximately 160 miles (260 km) west of Denver at an altitude of 7,132 feet and is within the Mountain Standard Time Zone (GMT+7).

In December 2023, Interior Secretary Deb Haaland designated Glenwood Caverns and Iron Mountain Hot Springs as a national landmark.

sections.title: Creation
sections.content: While hiking on Iron Mountain in the late 1800s, Charles W. Darrow followed a "whistling" sound created by natural air currents to discover the entrance to a cave system. In 1895, Darrow opened the caves to the public. The cave was originally only accessible by foot or horseback. In 1897, Darrow installed electric lights in the cave with the help of the city's hydro-electric plant, becoming one of the first caves to do so. In the same year, a tunnel was blasted out to Glenwood Canyon with an observation deck that was named Exclamation Point. In 1917, coinciding with the onset of World War I, the caves were closed to the public.

sections.title: New ownership
sections.content: By 1998, Steve and Jeanne Beckley came to own the land on which the cave is located. That year saw the beginning of the Fairy Caves Project, a volunteer organization coordinating caver access as well as science and conservation projects utilizing the land. This project allowed for the reopening of the cave to the public in 1999 after one year of restoration work. The caverns were accessible by bus via a station next to the Hotel Colorado. In 2002, the park was expanded, adding a pulse gondola built by Poma and a visitor center with a restaurant located atop the mountain. An aerial tramway, which allows year-round access to the caverns, a large sluice box, and a gift shop were also built.

sections.title: Development into Adventure Park
sections.content: After the caves were made more easily accessible by the aerial tramway, the wait time for the cave tours increased. Attractions were added to the park to provide patrons with entertainment while they were waiting for the cave tours. Major additions included North America's first alpine coaster, a zip ride, a swing-style attraction, and a climbing wall. All attractions opened in May 2005, and many of them were named by locals. To satisfy the increased demand, each pulse on the gondola received another cabin as well. From 2005 to 2010, various attractions were added, such as a 4-D theater—the first in Colorado—and other temporary features. The next major attraction came in 2010 in the form of the Giant Canyon Swing, a Screamin' Swing designed by S&S - Sansei Technologies. Around this time, the zip ride was relocated, sharing a joint tower structure with a bungee jump.

sections.title: Further expansions
sections.content: In 2012, the park undertook its largest expansion since 2005 with the addition of three rides. These included a Zierer children’s roller coaster and an SBF-Visa Ferris wheel, both relocated from Wild Zone Adventures in Canada. The primary addition was the Cliffhanger roller coaster, relocated from the former Celebration City amusement park in Branson, Missouri. The coaster opened in June 2012. It is the full-sized roller coaster with the highest elevation in the United States, operating at an elevation of 7,160 feet (2,180 m) above sea level.

sections.title: Cave and tramway improvements (2013)
sections.content: In 2013, the park expanded the upper portion of its cave system. As part of this project, the original cave tour was divided into two separate tours, both being approximately 40 minutes, which increased visitor capacity. That same year, the aerial tramway was expanded by adding two additional pulse sequences, bringing the total to six pulses and eighteen cabins.

sections.title: Additional rides (2014–2015)
sections.content: In 2014, the park installed the Glenwood Canyon Flyer, a custom SBF-Visa swing ride, as a less intense alternative to the Giant Canyon Swing. The ride was later featured on the television program Thrill Factor in 2015, in the episode "Kari Has No Sense."

sections.title: Haunted Mine Drop / Crystal Tower (2017)
sections.content: In early 2017, the park announced the addition of the Haunted Mine Drop, later renamed Crystal Tower. The ride opened in the summer of 2017. It is a drop tower that descends underground and features themed elements in addition to a 110-foot (34 m) vertical drop.

sections.title: Fatal ride accident
sections.content:
On the evening of September 5, 2021, six-year-old Wongel Estifanos died while riding the Haunted Mine Drop attraction after being separated from her seat and falling 10 ft. Investigators determined that Estifanos had been seated on top of the safety restraint rather than secured beneath it. A forensic pathologist identified the cause of death as blunt-force trauma. An official investigation by the Colorado Division of Oil and Public Safety cited inadequate procedures and insufficient training of two ride operators, who failed to ensure that Estifanos was properly restrained before the ride began. The Estifanos family was represented by attorney Dan Caplis in a subsequent civil lawsuit against the park, which resulted in a landmark jury verdict awarding $205 million in damages for the wrongful death of Wongel Estifanos.

Prior to the incident, the Haunted Mine Drop had undergone four annual safety inspections in accordance with Colorado Amusement Rides and Devices Regulations (7 CCR 1101–12) and had been certified as compliant, with prior issues reported as corrected. Following the fatal accident, Glenwood Caverns Adventure Park temporarily closed.

Under Colorado law, specifically Colorado Senate Bill 03-253, parents may waive a minor's right to sue for negligence, limiting legal options available to families in cases involving recreational activities. The Haunted Mine Drop remained closed until 2023, when it reopened as Crystal Tower.

Following this incident, the park filed for Chapter 11 bankruptcy on February 10, 2026, a year after a $116 million wrongful death judgment was tied against the company.

sections.title: Ride injuries
sections.content: Several injuries have also been reported on the Alpine Coaster attraction. In 2007, a parent and child were hospitalized after being thrown from the ride into a fence. In 2010, a 10-year-old child was ejected from the coaster and landed on a pile of rocks, sustaining facial injuries. In 2011, a woman suffered a broken back after the car she was riding in failed to brake for a series of stalled cars on the track. A lawsuit filed in connection with the incident was later dismissed.

sections.title: Security incident
sections.content: On October 28, 2023, prior to the park opening for the day, a 20-year-old Colorado man, identified as Diego Barajas Medina, was found dead inside the park from an apparent suicide. Medina was wearing tactical gear and was armed with improvised explosive devices and two privately made firearms, including a semi-automatic rifle and a handgun. Officials with the Garfield County Sheriff's Office stated that the weapons and equipment could have enabled a mass-casualty attack. A note discovered near Medina’s body, written on the wall of a women’s restroom stall, read, "I am not a killer. I just wanted to get into the caves."

fullText:
Glenwood Caverns Adventure Park is a summer-themed adventure park located near Glenwood Springs, Colorado. It is located approximately 160 miles (260 km) west of Denver at an altitude of 7,132 feet and is within the Mountain Standard Time Zone (GMT+7).

In December 2023, Interior Secretary Deb Haaland designated Glenwood Caverns and Iron Mountain Hot Springs as a national landmark.



While hiking on Iron Mountain in the late 1800s, Charles W. Darrow followed a "whistling" sound created by natural air currents to discover the entrance to a cave system. In 1895, Darrow opened the caves to the public. The cave was originally only accessible by foot or horseback. In 1897, Darrow installed electric lights in the cave with the help of the city's hydro-electric plant, becoming one of the first caves to do so. In the same year, a tunnel was blasted out to Glenwood Canyon with an observation deck that was named Exclamation Point. In 1917, coinciding with the onset of World War I, the caves were closed to the public.

By 1998, Steve and Jeanne Beckley came to own the land on which the cave is located. That year saw the beginning of the Fairy Caves Project, a volunteer organization coordinating caver access as well as science and conservation projects utilizing the land. This project allowed for the reopening of the cave to the public in 1999 after one year of restoration work. The caverns were accessible by bus via a station next to the Hotel Colorado. In 2002, the park was expanded, adding a pulse gondola built by Poma and a visitor center with a restaurant located atop the mountain. An aerial tramway, which allows year-round access to the caverns, a large sluice box, and a gift shop were also built.

After the caves were made more easily accessible by the aerial tramway, the wait time for the cave tours increased. Attractions were added to the park to provide patrons with entertainment while they were waiting for the cave tours. Major additions included North America's first alpine coaster, a zip ride, a swing-style attraction, and a climbing wall. All attractions opened in May 2005, and many of them were named by locals. To satisfy the increased demand, each pulse on the gondola received another cabin as well. From 2005 to 2010, various attractions were added, such as a 4-D theater—the first in Colorado—and other temporary features. The next major attraction came in 2010 in the form of the Giant Canyon Swing, a Screamin' Swing designed by S&S - Sansei Technologies. Around this time, the zip ride was relocated, sharing a joint tower structure with a bungee jump.

In 2012, the park undertook its largest expansion since 2005 with the addition of three rides. These included a Zierer children’s roller coaster and an SBF-Visa Ferris wheel, both relocated from Wild Zone Adventures in Canada. The primary addition was the Cliffhanger roller coaster, relocated from the former Celebration City amusement park in Branson, Missouri. The coaster opened in June 2012. It is the full-sized roller coaster with the highest elevation in the United States, operating at an elevation of 7,160 feet (2,180 m) above sea level.

In 2013, the park expanded the upper portion of its cave system. As part of this project, the original cave tour was divided into two separate tours, both being approximately 40 minutes, which increased visitor capacity. That same year, the aerial tramway was expanded by adding two additional pulse sequences, bringing the total to six pulses and eighteen cabins.

In 2014, the park installed the Glenwood Canyon Flyer, a custom SBF-Visa swing ride, as a less intense alternative to the Giant Canyon Swing. The ride was later featured on the television program Thrill Factor in 2015, in the episode "Kari Has No Sense."

In early 2017, the park announced the addition of the Haunted Mine Drop, later renamed Crystal Tower. The ride opened in the summer of 2017. It is a drop tower that descends underground and features themed elements in addition to a 110-foot (34 m) vertical drop.





On the evening of September 5, 2021, six-year-old Wongel Estifanos died while riding the Haunted Mine Drop attraction after being separated from her seat and falling 10 ft. Investigators determined that Estifanos had been seated on top of the safety restraint rather than secured beneath it. A forensic pathologist identified the cause of death as blunt-force trauma. An official investigation by the Colorado Division of Oil and Public Safety cited inadequate procedures and insufficient training of two ride operators, who failed to ensure that Estifanos was properly restrained before the ride began. The Estifanos family was represented by attorney Dan Caplis in a subsequent civil lawsuit against the park, which resulted in a landmark jury verdict awarding $205 million in damages for the wrongful death of Wongel Estifanos.

Prior to the incident, the Haunted Mine Drop had undergone four annual safety inspections in accordance with Colorado Amusement Rides and Devices Regulations (7 CCR 1101–12) and had been certified as compliant, with prior issues reported as corrected. Following the fatal accident, Glenwood Caverns Adventure Park temporarily closed.

Under Colorado law, specifically Colorado Senate Bill 03-253, parents may waive a minor's right to sue for negligence, limiting legal options available to families in cases involving recreational activities. The Haunted Mine Drop remained closed until 2023, when it reopened as Crystal Tower.

Following this incident, the park filed for Chapter 11 bankruptcy on February 10, 2026, a year after a $116 million wrongful death judgment was tied against the company.

Several injuries have also been reported on the Alpine Coaster attraction. In 2007, a parent and child were hospitalized after being thrown from the ride into a fence. In 2010, a 10-year-old child was ejected from the coaster and landed on a pile of rocks, sustaining facial injuries. In 2011, a woman suffered a broken back after the car she was riding in failed to brake for a series of stalled cars on the track. A lawsuit filed in connection with the incident was later dismissed.

On October 28, 2023, prior to the park opening for the day, a 20-year-old Colorado man, identified as Diego Barajas Medina, was found dead inside the park from an apparent suicide. Medina was wearing tactical gear and was armed with improvised explosive devices and two privately made firearms, including a semi-automatic rifle and a handgun. Officials with the Garfield County Sheriff's Office stated that the weapons and equipment could have enabled a mass-casualty attack. A note discovered near Medina’s body, written on the wall of a women’s restroom stall, read, "I am not a killer. I just wanted to get into the caves."




 * Glenwood Caverns website
